text: The surgeon uses a uterine manipulator to move the uterus to the contralateral side, revealing the avascular triangle of freedom for further dissection.
The surgeon uses a uterine manipulator to direct the uterus to the contralateral side, exposing the avascular triangle of freedom in the pelvic region.

The surgeon identifies anatomical boundaries of the avascular triangle of freedom, with the round ligament visible superiorly, the external iliac artery and vein laterally, and the gonadal vessels medially.
The anatomical area is defined by the round ligament superiorly, the external iliac artery and vein laterally, and the gonadal vessels medially.
The surgeon further exposes the avascular triangle of freedom by gently applying medial superior traction to the ovarian vessels, enhancing visibility of the surgical field.
The surgeon uses gentle medial superior traction to grasp and further expose the ovarian vessels within the avascular triangle of freedom.
The surgeon manipulates the uterus to create distance from the iliac vessels, resulting in visible peritoneal tenting.
The surgeon manipulates the uterus to create distance from the iliac vessels, resulting in visible peritoneal tenting.

The surgeon uses a gentle probe and spread technique to take superficial bites, further exposing the pillars and bridges of adipose areolar and peritoneal tissue. Counter traction is constantly adjusted to enhance visibility and access.

The surgeon uses a gentle probe and spread technique to take superficial bites, further exposing the pillars and bridges of adipose areolar and peritoneal tissue. The counter traction is constantly adjusted to enhance visibility and access.
The ureter is visible lying on the posterior peritoneum of the broad ligament, appearing increasingly superficial as it approaches the pelvic brim.
The ureter is visible lying on the posterior peritoneum of the broad ligament, appearing increasingly superficial as it approaches the pelvic brim.
The surgeon continues using the probe and spread technique to carefully approach the suspected location of the ureter, maintaining a clean dissection path through the exposed triangle of freedom.
The surgeon continues using the probe and spread technique to carefully approach the suspected location, maintaining a clean dissection path.

The surgeon identifies the location of the ureter and proceeds to take down the anterior peritoneum, extending the cranial peritoneal incision to further dissect the ureter and potentially create a pedicle window.

The surgeon identifies the ureter's location and proceeds to take down the anterior peritoneum, extending the cranial peritoneal incision to further dissect the ureter and potentially create a pedicle window.

The surgeon performs adhesiolysis on the left side of the pelvis to release congenital adhesions, allowing for medialization of the colon and exposure of the triangle of freedom for entry into the retroperitoneum.
The surgeon performs adhesiolysis on congenital adhesions on the left side of the pelvis to medialize the colon, exposing the triangle of freedom and facilitating entry into the retroperitoneum.
The surgeon grasps the mesentery of the colon, applying medial inferior countertraction to place tension on the adhesion, facilitating its visualization and manipulation.
The surgeon grasps the mesentery of the colon, applying medial inferior countertraction to place tension on the adhesion, facilitating its visualization and manipulation.
The surgeon uses bursts of monopolar energy while adjusting countertraction to safely medialize the bowel, manipulating the tissue to achieve the desired positioning.
The surgeon uses bursts of monopolar energy while adjusting countertraction to safely medialize the bowel, moving it towards the midline.
The surgeon has successfully medialized the bowel, exposing the triangle of freedom for safe entry into the retroperitoneal space.
The surgeon has successfully medialized the bowel, exposing the triangle of freedom for safe entry into the retroperitoneal space.

The surgeon uses a gentle probe and spread technique, adjusting the ovarian vessel countertraction to expose the iliac vessel landmarks. This maneuver reveals the ureter, which is located lateral and inferior to the iliac vessels.

The surgeon uses a gentle probe and spread technique, adjusting the ovarian vessel countertraction to expose the iliac vessel landmarks. This guides the dissection towards the ureter, which is located lateral and inferior to the iliac vessels.
The surgeon identifies the ureter located on the posterior peritoneum, visible as a tubular structure running laterally and inferiorly to the iliac vessels.
The surgeon identifies the ureter located on the posterior peritoneum, visible as a tubular structure running laterally and inferiorly to the iliac vessels.
The surgeon opens the anterior peritoneum parallel to the ovarian vessels, dissecting through the areolar tissue to expose and isolate the ureter.
The surgeon opens the anterior peritoneum parallel to the ovarian vessels, dissecting through the areolar tissue to expose the ureter.
The surgeon adjusts countertraction medial to the ovary, facilitating further caudal ureolysis by carefully dissecting around the ureter.
The surgeon adjusts countertraction medial to the ovary, facilitating further caudal ureolysis by carefully dissecting the ureter from surrounding tissues.
The uterine vessels are visible anterior to the ureter, originating from the hypogastric region.
The uterine vessels are visible anterior to the ureter, originating from the hypogastric artery.
The video shows the anatomical relationship where the ureter passes beneath the uterine vessels, illustrating the 'water under the bridge' concept.

The surgical field displays the development of the parietal space, with the ureter visible medially, the internal iliac artery laterally, and the uterine vessels positioned caudally.
The image displays the development of the parietal space, with the ureter visible medially, the internal iliac artery laterally, and the uterine vessels positioned caudally.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to skeletonize the uterine vessels, preparing them for safe ligation lateral to the ureter.
The surgeon uses blunt dissection to skeletonize the uterine vessels, preparing them for safe ligation lateral to the ureter.
Following the transection of the round ligament, the surgeon separately dissects the anterior and posterior peritoneum, facilitating continuous visual identification of the ureter.

Following the transection of the round ligament, the surgeon separately dissects the anterior and posterior peritoneum, facilitating clear identification of the ureter and enabling safe lateralization. This approach also enhances the skeletonization of the uterine artery.

The surgeon performs safe ureterolateralization, enhancing the visibility and separation of the ureter from surrounding tissues, while further skeletonizing the uterine artery for improved access and safety.
The surgeon uses the fourth arm prograsp to grasp the cornea of the uterus, incorporating the round ligament, and lifts the uterus out of the pelvis, visually exposing the triangle of freedom.

The surgeon uses the fourth arm prograsp to grasp the cornea of the uterus, incorporating the round ligament, and lifts the uterus out of the pelvis, exposing the triangle of freedom. The ovarian vessels are grasped with medial traction, and the retroperitoneum is entered, similar to the laparoscopic approach.
The surgeon uses medial traction to grasp the ovarian vessels and enters the retroperitoneum, similar to the laparoscopic approach.
The surgeon extends the incision parallel to the ovarian vessels, maintaining alignment with the previously exposed anatomical structures.
The surgeon extends the incision parallel to the ovarian vessels, maintaining alignment with the visible anatomical structures.
The surgeon identifies the medial leaf of the broad ligament and follows it inferiorly towards the ureter, ensuring clear visualization of the anatomical structures involved.

The surgeon identifies the ureter and extends the peritoneal incision, creating a peritoneal window medial to the ureter and lateral to the ovarian vessels. This allows for the safe ligation and transection of the ovarian vessels.

The surgeon extends the peritoneal incision and creates a peritoneal window medial to the identified ureter and lateral to the ovarian vessels, preparing to safely ligate and transect the ovarian vessels.
The surgeon prepares to take down the posterior aspect of the broad ligament, with the uterus lifted out of the pelvis to expose the surgical field.
The surgeon develops the vesicle vaginal space prior to taking down the posterior of the broad ligament, preparing the area for further dissection.
The surgeon develops the vesicle vaginal space, carefully separating the tissue layers to expose the anatomical area between the bladder and vagina.
The surgeon further dissects the ureter, ensuring it is a safe distance from the site of uterine artery ligation before transecting the posterior peritoneum.
The surgeon further dissects the ureter, ensuring it is at a safe distance from the uterine artery ligation site before transecting the posterior peritoneum.
